title: Greek Roast Lamb
subtitle: Leg of lamb with lemon, garlic, and oregano
excerpt: A whole leg of lamb studded with garlic, rubbed with oregano and lemon, and roasted over potatoes that absorb every drop of the juices. The Greek Sunday lunch.
sourceNotes: Greek sto fourno (baked in the oven) lamb is the canonical Easter and Sunday dish, documented in Vefa Alexiadou, Greek Cuisine (1991) and Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001). The combination of lemon, oregano, garlic, and olive oil is consistent across all Greek versions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The lamb can be studded and rubbed the night before and kept covered in the fridge. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before roasting.

content.text: Greek roast lamb is defined by three things: garlic stuffed into slits in the meat so it melts and perfumes the flesh from inside, a generous rub of dried oregano and lemon, and potatoes positioned beneath the joint so they roast in the falling juices. The result is lamb that smells of the Mediterranean and potatoes that are better than any potato has a right to be.

The cooking time here gives pink-blushed meat. If you want well-done, as many Greek households prefer, add 30 to 40 minutes and keep the tray covered for the first hour. Either way, rest the lamb for at least 20 minutes before carving — a leg this size needs it.

items.prepNote: 4 sliced into slivers for stuffing, 4 crushed for the paste
for the bottom of the pan

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Use a thin sharp knife to cut deep slits all over the lamb — about 20 to 25 slits, roughly 2 cm deep. Push a sliver of garlic into each slit. Mix the crushed garlic, lemon zest and juice, olive oil, oregano, rosemary, salt, and pepper into a paste. Rub this all over the lamb, pressing it into the slits.

Spread the potato wedges in the roasting tin if using. Season them with salt, pepper, and a little olive oil. Place the lamb on top. Pour 200 ml of water into the base of the tin. Cover tightly with foil and roast for 1 hour. Remove the foil, baste the lamb and potatoes with the pan juices, and roast uncovered for a further 40 minutes until the lamb is browned and the internal temperature reads 60°C for pink or 75°C for well-done.

Transfer the lamb to a board, cover loosely with foil, and rest for at least 20 minutes. The potatoes can stay in the turned-off oven. Carve and serve with the potatoes, a Greek salad, and bread to mop the juices.

items.cause: Not enough liquid in the pan or cut too thick
items.fix: Cut wedges no thicker than 3 cm and add more water to the pan; they need steam to cook through

items.symptom: Garlic burns before the lamb is cooked
items.cause: Garlic on the surface burns; garlic stuffed inside doesn't
items.fix: Push the garlic slivers fully into the slits; surface garlic can be scraped off if it darkens too much

items.cause: Overcooked; or not rested long enough
items.fix: Roast to 60°C internal for pink, rest for 20 minutes minimum — the temperature continues to rise during resting

content.text: Greek roast lamb is the Easter dish. On Megali Paraskevi (Good Friday) and through the Easter weekend, ovens across Greece hold variations of this: whole leg, shoulder, or the whole animal on a spit. The oven version (sto fourno) is the domestic form, the one cooked every Sunday in apartments without gardens. Leftover lamb folded into pitta with tzatziki the next day is arguably the better meal.
